name: Diabetes
description: Diabetes is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by elevated blood sugar levels. It affects how the body processes glucose, the main source of energy. Clinical trials for diabetes explore new treatments, insulin delivery systems, and potential cures.
symptoms: Increased thirst, frequent urination, extreme hunger, unexplained weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision, slow-healing sores
riskFactors: Family history, obesity, physical inactivity, age, high blood pressure, abnormal cholesterol levels
treatment: Diabetes management includes insulin therapy, oral medications, lifestyle modifications, blood sugar monitoring, and dietary changes. Clinical trials are investigating new drug classes, artificial pancreas systems, and regenerative medicine approaches.
whyParticipate: Participating in diabetes clinical trials provides access to cutting-edge treatments, contributes to medical research, and helps future generations manage this condition more effectively.

faq.q: What are diabetes clinical trials?
faq.a: Diabetes clinical trials are research studies that test new treatments, medications, devices, or interventions for managing or curing diabetes. They help determine the safety and effectiveness of potential new approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in diabetes clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility varies by study. Factors include type of diabetes (Type 1, Type 2, gestational), age, duration of diagnosis, current treatment regimen, and overall health status. Each study has specific inclusion and exclusion criteria.

faq.q: How much do diabetes clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation depends on the study duration, number of visits, and procedures involved. Participants may receive payments ranging from $50 to $300 per visit, with total compensation varying by study.

faq.q: Are diabetes clinical trials safe?
faq.a: All clinical trials follow strict safety protocols and are reviewed by Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) to protect participants. Potential risks are explained during the informed consent process before enrollment.

name: Cancer
description: Cancer is a broad group of diseases characterized by abnormal cell growth with the potential to invade other parts of the body. Clinical trials in oncology are crucial for developing new treatments including immunotherapies, targeted therapies, and precision medicine approaches.
symptoms: Unexplained weight loss, persistent fatigue, lumps or swelling, changes in skin, persistent cough, changes in bowel or bladder habits
riskFactors: Tobacco use, excessive alcohol consumption, poor diet, physical inactivity, genetic predisposition, exposure to carcinogens, age
treatment: Cancer treatment varies by type and stage and includes surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, hormone therapy, and targeted drug therapy. Clinical trials are at the forefront of discovering more effective and less toxic treatment options.
whyParticipate: Cancer clinical trials offer access to promising new therapies before they become widely available and contribute directly to advancing cancer research and treatment standards.

faq.q: What are cancer clinical trials?
faq.a: Cancer clinical trials are research studies that evaluate new treatments, diagnostic methods, or prevention strategies. They are essential for improving cancer care and finding potential cures.

faq.q: Who can participate in cancer clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on cancer type, stage, prior treatments, and overall health. Each study specifies criteria including tumor type, genetic markers, and performance status.

faq.q: How much do cancer clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Many cancer trials cover travel expenses and provide stipends. The focus is typically on treatment access rather than high compensation.

faq.q: Are cancer clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Patient safety is paramount. Trials follow rigorous protocols reviewed by ethics committees, and participants are closely monitored throughout the study.

name: Depression
description: Depression (major depressive disorder) is a common mood disorder that causes persistent sadness, loss of interest, and impaired daily functioning. Clinical trials for depression investigate new antidepressant medications, neuromodulation therapies, and psychotherapeutic approaches.
symptoms: Persistent sadness, loss of interest in activities, changes in appetite, sleep disturbances, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, feelings of worthlessness, suicidal thoughts
riskFactors: Family history, trauma or stress, certain medical conditions, substance use, personality factors, hormonal changes
treatment: Treatment includes antidepressant medications, psychotherapy (CBT, interpersonal therapy), electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), and lifestyle modifications. Clinical trials explore novel mechanisms of action and personalized treatment approaches.
whyParticipate: Depression clinical trials provide access to innovative treatments and contribute to understanding the biological basis of mood disorders, potentially helping millions who do not respond to existing therapies.

faq.q: What are depression clinical trials?
faq.a: Depression clinical trials investigate new medications, therapies, or devices to treat depression. They aim to find more effective and faster-acting treatments with fewer side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in depression clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a diagnosis of depression based on standardized criteria. Factors include severity, treatment history, age, and absence of certain other conditions.

faq.q: How much do depression clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study intensity. Outpatient studies may pay $50-$150 per visit, while more intensive studies may offer higher compensation.

faq.q: Are depression clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials have safety monitoring. Participants are carefully screened and monitored throughout. The informed consent process explains all potential risks and benefits.

name: Obesity
description: Obesity is a complex medical condition characterized by excessive body fat that increases health risks. Clinical trials for obesity explore weight management medications, surgical techniques, behavioral interventions, and combination approaches.
symptoms: Excessive body weight, difficulty with physical activity, shortness of breath, joint pain, fatigue, sleep apnea
riskFactors: Genetic predisposition, poor diet, physical inactivity, certain medications, medical conditions, environmental factors, lack of sleep
treatment: Treatment includes dietary changes, increased physical activity, behavior modification, prescription medications, and bariatric surgery. Clinical trials are investigating new pharmacotherapies, gut microbiome interventions, and digital health tools.
whyParticipate: Obesity clinical trials offer access to emerging treatments and contribute to understanding the complex mechanisms of weight regulation, helping address the global obesity epidemic.

faq.q: What are obesity clinical trials?
faq.a: Obesity clinical trials test new medications, devices, surgical techniques, and lifestyle interventions for weight loss and weight management.

faq.q: Who can participate in obesity clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility usually requires a certain BMI range. Studies may focus on specific populations such as those with obesity-related complications or those seeking non-surgical options.

faq.q: How much do obesity clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Studies may offer $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Longer-term studies with more visits typically offer higher compensation.

faq.q: Are obesity clinical trials safe?
faq.a: All trials follow strict safety guidelines. Participants receive medical supervision throughout the study period and are informed of all potential risks before enrollment.

name: Heart Disease
description: Heart disease encompasses various conditions affecting the heart and blood vessels, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, and arrhythmias. Clinical trials investigate new medications, devices, surgical techniques, and prevention strategies.
symptoms: Chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, irregular heartbeat, dizziness, swelling in legs, fainting
riskFactors: High blood pressure, high cholesterol, smoking, diabetes, obesity, family history, age, sedentary lifestyle
treatment: Treatment includes medications (statins, beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors), lifestyle changes, angioplasty, stent placement, bypass surgery, and cardiac rehabilitation. Clinical trials explore novel therapies and minimally invasive procedures.
whyParticipate: Heart disease clinical trials advance cardiovascular medicine and provide access to innovative treatments that may improve quality of life and outcomes for millions affected by heart conditions.

faq.q: What are heart disease clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials investigate new treatments, medications, devices, and prevention strategies for various heart conditions, from coronary artery disease to heart failure.

faq.q: Who can participate in heart disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the specific heart condition being studied, its severity, previous treatments, and overall health status.

faq.q: How much do heart disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study complexity and duration. Participants may receive $100-$300 per visit plus travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are heart disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Safety is carefully monitored with regular checkups and assessments. All trials have independent oversight to protect participant wellbeing.

name: Asthma
description: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition where airways become inflamed and narrow, causing breathing difficulty. Clinical trials explore new inhaler technologies, biologic therapies, and personalized management approaches.
symptoms: Wheezing, coughing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, symptoms worsen at night or with exercise
riskFactors: Family history, allergies, respiratory infections, occupational exposures, smoking, air pollution, obesity
treatment: Treatment includes inhaled corticosteroids, bronchodilators, leukotriene modifiers, and biologic therapies. Clinical trials investigate novel biologic targets, improved delivery devices, and precision medicine approaches.
whyParticipate: Asthma clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with fewer side effects and contribute to understanding the underlying mechanisms of airway inflammation.

faq.q: What are asthma clinical trials?
faq.a: Asthma clinical trials test new medications, inhaler devices, and management strategies to improve symptom control and reduce exacerbations.

faq.q: Who can participate in asthma clinical trials?
faq.a: Studies typically require a confirmed asthma diagnosis. Factors include severity, current medications, age, and trigger profile.

faq.q: How much do asthma clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation typically ranges from $50-$150 per visit. Study duration and number of visits affect total compensation.

faq.q: Are asthma clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are closely monitored with regular lung function tests and assessments. Rescue medications are typically provided during the study.

name: Chronic Pain
description: Chronic pain is persistent pain lasting beyond normal healing time, affecting physical function and quality of life. Clinical trials investigate non-opioid pain management options, nerve stimulation therapies, and integrative approaches.
symptoms: Persistent pain lasting more than 3-6 months, fatigue, sleep disturbances, mood changes, reduced mobility, decreased quality of life
riskFactors: Previous injury, surgery, arthritis, nerve damage, fibromyalgia, age, obesity, stress, genetic predisposition
treatment: Treatment includes physical therapy, medications (NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, antidepressants), nerve blocks, spinal cord stimulation, cognitive behavioral therapy, and acupuncture. Clinical trials explore novel non-addictive pain medications and neuromodulation techniques.
whyParticipate: Chronic pain clinical trials are essential for developing safer alternatives to opioids and improving pain management strategies for millions suffering from persistent pain conditions.

faq.q: What are chronic pain clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials investigate new treatments for persistent pain conditions, including medications, nerve stimulation, physical therapy approaches, and psychological interventions.

faq.q: Who can participate in chronic pain clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on pain type, duration, location, and prior treatments. Studies may focus on specific conditions like back pain or fibromyalgia.

faq.q: How much do chronic pain clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Participants typically receive $75-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are chronic pain clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, trials follow strict protocols with regular monitoring. Pain management is always available, and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Anxiety
description: Anxiety disorders involve excessive fear, worry, and related behavioral disturbances that interfere with daily life. Clinical trials investigate new medications, digital therapeutics, and psychotherapy approaches for anxiety treatment.
symptoms: Excessive worry, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, sleep problems, panic attacks, avoidance behaviors
riskFactors: Family history, childhood adversity, stress, certain medical conditions, substance use, personality factors
treatment: Treatment includes SSRIs, SNRIs, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), mindfulness-based approaches, and exposure therapy. Clinical trials explore psychedelic-assisted therapy, novel anxiolytics, and digital mental health interventions.
whyParticipate: Anxiety clinical trials contribute to developing more effective, faster-acting treatments and help reduce the burden of anxiety disorders affecting millions worldwide.

faq.q: What are anxiety clinical trials?
faq.a: Anxiety clinical trials test new medications, therapies, and interventions to reduce anxiety symptoms and improve daily functioning.

faq.q: Who can participate in anxiety clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a diagnosed anxiety disorder. Factors include severity, duration, previous treatments, and absence of certain other conditions.

faq.q: How much do anxiety clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation typically ranges from $50-$150 per visit. Remote or digital therapy studies may offer different compensation structures.

faq.q: Are anxiety clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive thorough screening and ongoing monitoring. Therapeutic support is available throughout the trial period.

name: High Blood Pressure
description: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is a common condition where blood vessel pressure is consistently elevated, increasing risk for heart disease and stroke. Clinical trials explore new antihypertensive medications, device-based therapies, and lifestyle interventions.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic, but may include headaches, shortness of breath, nosebleeds, dizziness, chest pain, vision changes
riskFactors: Age, family history, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, high sodium intake, alcohol consumption, stress, kidney disease
treatment: Treatment includes lifestyle modifications (diet, exercise, sodium reduction) and medications (diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers). Clinical trials investigate novel drug targets and renal denervation techniques.
whyParticipate: High blood pressure clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with fewer side effects and advance understanding of hypertension mechanisms.

faq.q: What are high blood pressure clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials investigate new medications, devices, and lifestyle approaches to lower blood pressure and reduce cardiovascular risk.

faq.q: Who can participate in high blood pressure clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on blood pressure levels, current medications, and overall health status. Some studies focus on resistant hypertension.

faq.q: How much do high blood pressure clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are high blood pressure clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, blood pressure is closely monitored throughout trials. Participants receive regular checkups and can withdraw at any time.

name: Alzheimer's Disease
description: Alzheimer's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that causes memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioral changes. Clinical trials investigate disease-modifying therapies, early detection methods, and interventions to slow progression.
symptoms: Memory loss, difficulty with familiar tasks, confusion with time or place, trouble understanding visual images, problems with speaking or writing, changes in personality
riskFactors: Age, family history, genetic factors (APOE-e4), head injury, cardiovascular risk factors, limited education, social isolation
treatment: Current treatments include cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine to manage symptoms. Clinical trials are exploring anti-amyloid antibodies, tau-targeting therapies, neuroprotective agents, and lifestyle interventions.
whyParticipate: Alzheimer's clinical trials are critical for finding treatments that can slow or prevent disease progression, offering hope to millions affected by this devastating condition.

faq.q: What are Alzheimer's clinical trials?
faq.a: Alzheimer's clinical trials test new medications, interventions, and diagnostic tools to treat, slow, or prevent the progression of Alzheimer's disease.

faq.q: Who can participate in Alzheimer's clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility varies by study. Some focus on early-stage Alzheimer's, others on mild cognitive impairment, and some include healthy volunteers at risk.

faq.q: How much do Alzheimer's clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Studies may offer $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Caregiver support is sometimes provided.

faq.q: Are Alzheimer's clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, safety is the top priority. Trials have independent monitoring, regular assessments, and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Parkinson's Disease
description: Parkinson's disease is a progressive neurological disorder affecting movement, balance, and coordination due to loss of dopamine-producing brain cells. Clinical trials explore neuroprotective therapies, symptom management innovations, and potential disease-modifying treatments.
symptoms: Tremor, bradykinesia (slowness of movement), rigidity, postural instability, changes in speech, loss of automatic movements, cognitive changes
riskFactors: Age, genetic factors, environmental exposures, head injury, male gender, family history
treatment: Treatment includes levodopa/carbidopa, dopamine agonists, MAO-B inhibitors, deep brain stimulation (DBS), and physical therapy. Clinical trials investigate gene therapies, stem cell treatments, and novel drug delivery systems.
whyParticipate: Parkinson's clinical trials accelerate the development of treatments that could slow disease progression and improve quality of life for those living with this condition.

faq.q: What are Parkinson's disease clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials test new treatments, medications, and therapies to manage Parkinson's symptoms and potentially slow or halt disease progression.

faq.q: Who can participate in Parkinson's clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on disease stage, current medications, age, and overall health. Some studies focus on early-stage disease while others include advanced cases.

faq.q: How much do Parkinson's clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with travel reimbursement for extended study commitments.

faq.q: Are Parkinson's clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials follow strict safety protocols with regular monitoring. Participants are informed of all risks before enrollment.

name: COPD
description: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is a progressive lung disease causing airflow blockage and breathing difficulties. Clinical trials investigate new bronchodilators, anti-inflammatory therapies, and interventions to improve lung function and quality of life.
symptoms: Chronic cough, shortness of breath, wheezing, chest tightness, excess mucus production, frequent respiratory infections, fatigue
riskFactors: Smoking, secondhand smoke exposure, occupational dusts and chemicals, air pollution, genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency), age
treatment: Treatment includes bronchodilators, inhaled corticosteroids, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy, and lung volume reduction surgery. Clinical trials explore novel biologic therapies, stem cell treatments, and digital health monitoring.
whyParticipate: COPD clinical trials are essential for developing more effective treatments that can slow disease progression and improve breathing for millions affected worldwide.

faq.q: What are COPD clinical trials?
faq.a: COPD clinical trials test new medications, devices, and rehabilitation strategies to improve lung function, reduce exacerbations, and enhance quality of life.

faq.q: Who can participate in COPD clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a confirmed COPD diagnosis with specific lung function measurements. Factors include smoking history, exacerbation frequency, and current medications.

faq.q: How much do COPD clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit plus travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are COPD clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular pulmonary function tests and medical monitoring. Rescue medications are provided and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Arthritis
description: Arthritis is inflammation of one or more joints causing pain and stiffness that worsens with age. Clinical trials investigate new anti-inflammatory medications, biologic therapies, joint preservation techniques, and regenerative medicine approaches.
symptoms: Joint pain, stiffness, swelling, decreased range of motion, redness around joints, warmth, fatigue
riskFactors: Age, family history, obesity, previous joint injury, autoimmune conditions, infection, occupation
treatment: Treatment includes NSAIDs, corticosteroids, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), physical therapy, and joint replacement surgery. Clinical trials explore novel JAK inhibitors, biosimilars, and cartilage regeneration therapies.
whyParticipate: Arthritis clinical trials help develop more effective pain management options and disease-modifying treatments that can preserve joint function and improve quality of life.

faq.q: What are arthritis clinical trials?
faq.a: Arthritis clinical trials test new medications, therapies, and interventions to reduce joint inflammation, manage pain, and prevent joint damage.

faq.q: Who can participate in arthritis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on arthritis type (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, etc.), severity, previous treatments, and overall health.

faq.q: How much do arthritis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are arthritis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials have safety monitoring. Participants receive regular checkups and are informed of all potential risks during the consent process.

name: Migraine
description: Migraine is a neurological condition characterized by recurrent moderate to severe headaches often accompanied by sensory disturbances. Clinical trials investigate CGRP inhibitors, neuromodulation devices, and novel acute and preventive treatments.
symptoms: Throbbing head pain (often one-sided), nausea, vomiting, sensitivity to light and sound, visual aura, dizziness, cognitive difficulty
riskFactors: Family history, gender (more common in women), hormonal changes, stress, certain foods and drinks, sleep changes, weather changes
treatment: Treatment includes acute medications (triptans, NSAIDs, gepants) and preventive treatments (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP monoclonal antibodies, Botox). Clinical trials explore new classes of acute and preventive therapies.
whyParticipate: Migraine clinical trials contribute to developing more effective treatments with fewer side effects and help understand the underlying mechanisms of this complex neurological condition.

faq.q: What are migraine clinical trials?
faq.a: Migraine clinical trials test new medications, devices, and therapies to prevent migraines or treat them more effectively when they occur.

faq.q: Who can participate in migraine clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a diagnosis of migraine (with or without aura). Factors include frequency, duration, and previous treatment responses.

faq.q: How much do migraine clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $75-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Some trials also provide study medication.

faq.q: Are migraine clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are closely monitored throughout the study. Rescue medications are typically available, and safety protocols are strictly followed.

name: COVID-19
description: COVID-19 is an infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Clinical trials continue to investigate treatments, vaccines, and long-term management strategies for acute infection and long COVID symptoms.
symptoms: Fever, cough, fatigue, loss of taste or smell, shortness of breath, body aches, sore throat, congestion, headache
riskFactors: Age, immunocompromised status, underlying medical conditions (heart disease, diabetes, obesity), smoking, pregnancy, unvaccinated status
treatment: Treatment includes antiviral medications, monoclonal antibodies, supportive care, and vaccination for prevention. Clinical trials explore new antiviral agents, combination therapies, and interventions for long COVID.
whyParticipate: COVID-19 clinical trials are vital for developing better treatments, understanding long-term effects, and preparing for future pandemic threats.

faq.q: What are COVID-19 clinical trials?
faq.a: COVID-19 clinical trials investigate new treatments, vaccines, and management strategies for acute infection and long-term complications of the disease.

faq.q: Who can participate in COVID-19 clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility varies by study. Some focus on acute treatment, others on prevention, and many are studying long COVID management.

faq.q: How much do COVID-19 clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study type and duration. Vaccine trials and treatment studies typically offer $50-$200 per visit.

faq.q: Are COVID-19 clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials follow strict safety protocols. Participants are monitored closely and can withdraw at any time.

name: ADHD
description: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder affecting attention, impulse control, and activity levels. Clinical trials investigate new medications, behavioral interventions, and digital therapeutic approaches.
symptoms: Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity, difficulty organizing tasks, forgetfulness, restlessness, interrupting others, difficulty waiting turn
riskFactors: Genetics, premature birth, low birth weight, prenatal exposure to substances, brain injury, environmental toxins
treatment: Treatment includes stimulant and non-stimulant medications, behavioral therapy, organizational skills training, and educational support. Clinical trials explore novel non-stimulant medications, digital therapeutics, and neuromodulation.
whyParticipate: ADHD clinical trials help develop more treatment options with fewer side effects and improve understanding of ADHD across different age groups.

faq.q: What are ADHD clinical trials?
faq.a: ADHD clinical trials test new medications, behavioral interventions, and digital tools to improve attention, reduce hyperactivity, and enhance daily functioning.

faq.q: Who can participate in ADHD clinical trials?
faq.a: Studies include children, adolescents, and adults with diagnosed ADHD. Eligibility depends on age, symptom profile, and treatment history.

faq.q: How much do ADHD clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Studies typically offer $50-$150 per visit. Child studies may include parent compensation for time and travel.

faq.q: Are ADHD clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are closely monitored. All trials have independent oversight, and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Autism
description: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental condition affecting communication, social interaction, and behavior. Clinical trials investigate behavioral interventions, medications for associated symptoms, and support strategies across the lifespan.
symptoms: Difficulty with social communication, restricted interests, repetitive behaviors, sensory sensitivities, difficulty with changes in routine, varying language abilities
riskFactors: Genetic factors, advanced parental age, preterm birth, prenatal exposure to certain medications, family history
treatment: Treatment is individualized and may include behavioral therapy (ABA), speech therapy, occupational therapy, social skills training, and medications for co-occurring conditions. Clinical trials explore targeted therapies for core symptoms.
whyParticipate: Autism clinical trials advance understanding of the condition and help develop more effective interventions and support strategies for individuals across the autism spectrum.

faq.q: What are autism clinical trials?
faq.a: Autism clinical trials test behavioral interventions, medications, and therapies to improve communication, social skills, and quality of life for individuals with autism.

faq.q: Who can participate in autism clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on age, autism diagnosis confirmation, and specific study goals. Some studies include children, others focus on adults.

faq.q: How much do autism clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Studies may offer $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Behavioral studies may have different compensation structures.

faq.q: Are autism clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials prioritize participant safety with regular monitoring and ethical oversight. The consent process explains all procedures and risks.

name: PTSD
description: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is a mental health condition triggered by experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event. Clinical trials investigate trauma-focused therapies, medications, and novel treatments including psychedelic-assisted therapy.
symptoms: Intrusive memories, flashbacks, nightmares, severe anxiety, avoidance of trauma reminders, negative thoughts, hypervigilance, irritability, sleep disturbances
riskFactors: Experiencing trauma, childhood adversity, lack of social support, family history of mental health conditions, personality factors, occupation (military, first responders)
treatment: Treatment includes trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, prolonged exposure therapy, SSRIs, and SNRIs. Clinical trials explore MDMA-assisted therapy, ketamine, and novel psychotherapeutic approaches.
whyParticipate: PTSD clinical trials are essential for developing more effective treatments for those who do not respond to existing therapies, particularly in military and first responder populations.

faq.q: What are PTSD clinical trials?
faq.a: PTSD clinical trials test new therapeutic approaches, medications, and interventions to reduce trauma-related symptoms and improve daily functioning.

faq.q: Who can participate in PTSD clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a PTSD diagnosis based on standardized criteria. Studies may focus on specific trauma types or populations such as veterans.

faq.q: How much do PTSD clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit. Some intensive therapy studies offer higher compensation.

faq.q: Are PTSD clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participant safety is paramount. Trials include regular mental health monitoring, and therapeutic support is available throughout the study.

name: Kidney Disease
description: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is a progressive loss of kidney function over time. Clinical trials investigate treatments to slow disease progression, manage complications, and improve outcomes for dialysis and transplant patients.
symptoms: Fatigue, swelling in legs and ankles, shortness of breath, nausea, confusion, difficulty sleeping, muscle cramps, changes in urination
riskFactors: Diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, family history, age, obesity, smoking, certain ethnicities
treatment: Treatment includes medications (ACE inhibitors, SGLT2 inhibitors), dietary modifications, dialysis, and kidney transplantation. Clinical trials explore novel nephroprotective agents and artificial kidney technologies.
whyParticipate: Kidney disease clinical trials advance treatment options that can slow disease progression, reduce dialysis dependence, and improve outcomes for patients with compromised kidney function.

faq.q: What are kidney disease clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials investigate new treatments, medications, and devices to preserve kidney function, manage complications, and improve dialysis and transplant outcomes.

faq.q: Who can participate in kidney disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on CKD stage, underlying cause, current treatments, and overall health. Some studies focus on early-stage disease, others on dialysis patients.

faq.q: How much do kidney disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement for study-related appointments.

faq.q: Are kidney disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring throughout the trial. All studies have independent safety oversight and ethics committee approval.

name: Sleep Disorders
description: Sleep disorders encompass conditions affecting sleep quality, timing, and duration including insomnia, sleep apnea, and narcolepsy. Clinical trials investigate new medications, behavioral interventions, and devices to improve sleep health.
symptoms: Difficulty falling or staying asleep, excessive daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, gasping during sleep, restless legs, irregular sleep-wake cycles, fatigue
riskFactors: Stress, irregular schedule, certain medical conditions, medications, age, obesity, family history, mental health conditions
treatment: Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), CPAP therapy, oral appliances, medications, and lifestyle modifications. Clinical trials explore novel hypnotics, orexin antagonists, and digital sleep interventions.
whyParticipate: Sleep disorder clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with fewer side effects and advance understanding of the critical role of sleep in overall health.

faq.q: What are sleep disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Sleep disorder clinical trials test new medications, devices, and behavioral interventions to improve sleep quality, duration, and daytime functioning.

faq.q: Who can participate in sleep disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the specific sleep condition, severity, and prior treatments. Sleep studies often include overnight monitoring.

faq.q: How much do sleep disorder clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Overnight sleep studies may offer $200-$500+ per night. Outpatient behavioral studies may offer $50-$150 per visit.

faq.q: Are sleep disorder clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are monitored throughout the study. Sleep studies are conducted in controlled environments with medical supervision.

name: Liver Disease
description: Liver disease encompasses conditions causing liver damage including fatty liver disease, hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver cancer. Clinical trials investigate antiviral therapies, antifibrotic agents, and treatments for metabolic liver conditions.
symptoms: Jaundice, abdominal pain and swelling, chronic fatigue, nausea, loss of appetite, easy bruising, dark urine, pale stools
riskFactors: Excessive alcohol consumption, obesity, diabetes, viral hepatitis (B and C), genetic factors, certain medications, exposure to toxins
treatment: Treatment depends on the underlying cause and includes antiviral medications, lifestyle modifications, corticosteroids, and liver transplantation. Clinical trials explore NASH treatments, gene therapies, and novel antifibrotic agents.
whyParticipate: Liver disease clinical trials are crucial for developing treatments for conditions like NASH and cirrhosis where few therapeutic options currently exist.

faq.q: What are liver disease clinical trials?
faq.a: These trials investigate new treatments for various liver conditions including fatty liver disease, hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver cancer.

faq.q: Who can participate in liver disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the specific liver condition, disease stage, prior treatments, and overall health status.

faq.q: How much do liver disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement.

faq.q: Are liver disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials follow strict safety guidelines with regular liver function monitoring and independent oversight.

name: Psoriasis
description: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition causing rapid skin cell buildup resulting in scaly patches. Clinical trials investigate biologic therapies, topical treatments, and systemic medications to control inflammation and improve skin clearance.
symptoms: Red, raised, inflamed patches of skin, silvery-white scales, dry cracked skin, itching, burning, thickened or ridged nails, joint swelling (psoriatic arthritis)
riskFactors: Family history, immune system disorders, stress, infections, certain medications, smoking, obesity
treatment: Treatment includes topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs, phototherapy, oral systemic medications, and biologic therapies targeting specific immune pathways. Clinical trials explore novel cytokine inhibitors and oral small molecules.
whyParticipate: Psoriasis clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with convenient dosing and contribute to understanding autoimmune mechanisms.

faq.q: What are psoriasis clinical trials?
faq.a: Psoriasis clinical trials test new medications, biologic therapies, and topical treatments to reduce skin inflammation and improve quality of life.

faq.q: Who can participate in psoriasis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on psoriasis severity, body surface area affected, prior treatments, and whether psoriatic arthritis is present.

faq.q: How much do psoriasis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus study-related medications and monitoring at no cost.

faq.q: Are psoriasis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are carefully monitored for side effects. All trials have safety oversight and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Hepatitis C
description: Hepatitis C is a viral infection causing liver inflammation that can become chronic and lead to cirrhosis or liver cancer. Clinical trials investigate new antiviral therapies, treatment-shortening regimens, and approaches for hard-to-treat populations.
symptoms: Fatigue, fever, nausea, poor appetite, abdominal pain, dark urine, clay-colored stools, joint pain, jaundice
riskFactors: IV drug use, blood transfusion before 1992, needlestick injuries, hemodialysis, HIV co-infection, born to infected mother
treatment: Treatment includes direct-acting antiviral (DAA) medications that can cure most cases. Clinical trials explore pangenotypic regimens, shorter treatment durations, and strategies for eliminating hepatitis C globally.
whyParticipate: Hepatitis C clinical trials help develop more accessible and shorter treatment regimens, contributing to the global goal of eliminating hepatitis C as a public health threat.

faq.q: What are hepatitis C clinical trials?
faq.a: Hepatitis C clinical trials test new antiviral medications and treatment approaches to improve cure rates and reduce treatment duration.

faq.q: Who can participate in hepatitis C clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on hepatitis C genotype, viral load, liver function, prior treatment history, and whether cirrhosis is present.

faq.q: How much do hepatitis C clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are hepatitis C clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring of liver function and viral levels. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: HIV/AIDS
description: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) attacks the immune system and can progress to AIDS without treatment. Clinical trials investigate long-acting antiretroviral therapies, vaccine development, and cure research strategies.
symptoms: Fever, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, rash, muscle aches, sore throat, night sweats, mouth ulcers, weight loss
riskFactors: Unprotected sex, sharing needles, mother-to-child transmission, blood transfusion (rare), occupational exposure
treatment: Treatment includes antiretroviral therapy (ART) which controls viral replication and prevents progression to AIDS. Clinical trials explore long-acting injectable formulations, broadly neutralizing antibodies, and HIV cure strategies.
whyParticipate: HIV clinical trials are essential for developing longer-acting treatments, improving prevention methods, and working toward a functional cure for HIV.

faq.q: What are HIV clinical trials?
faq.a: HIV clinical trials test new antiretroviral medications, prevention methods (PrEP), vaccine candidates, and potential cure strategies.

faq.q: Who can participate in HIV clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility varies. Some studies include people living with HIV, others focus on HIV-negative individuals for prevention research.

faq.q: How much do HIV clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are HIV clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, all trials have strict safety protocols. Participants receive regular health monitoring and can access standard HIV care throughout the study.

name: Multiple Sclerosis
description: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the protective myelin sheath covering nerve fibers. Clinical trials investigate disease-modifying therapies, remyelination strategies, and symptomatic treatments.
symptoms: Fatigue, numbness or tingling, weakness, vision problems, balance issues, bladder dysfunction, cognitive changes, spasticity, pain
riskFactors: Age (20-40), female gender, family history, certain infections (Epstein-Barr), vitamin D deficiency, smoking, obesity, Northern latitude
treatment: Treatment includes disease-modifying therapies (interferons, glatiramer acetate, S1P receptor modulators, monoclonal antibodies), corticosteroids for relapses, and symptomatic treatments. Clinical trials explore BTK inhibitors, remyelinating agents, and cell-based therapies.
whyParticipate: MS clinical trials are vital for developing treatments that can stop disease progression, repair nerve damage, and improve quality of life for those living with MS.

faq.q: What are multiple sclerosis clinical trials?
faq.a: MS clinical trials test new medications and therapies to reduce relapses, slow disease progression, and manage symptoms of multiple sclerosis.

faq.q: Who can participate in MS clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on MS type (relapsing-remitting, primary progressive, secondary progressive), disease activity, and prior treatments.

faq.q: How much do MS clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Study medications and monitoring are typically provided.

faq.q: Are MS clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular neurological assessments and monitoring. All trials have independent safety committees and ethical oversight.

name: Osteoporosis
description: Osteoporosis is a condition where bones become weak and brittle due to loss of bone density. Clinical trials investigate new medications to increase bone density, reduce fracture risk, and improve bone quality.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic until fracture, back pain, loss of height over time, stooped posture, bone fractures from minor falls
riskFactors: Age, female gender, family history, low body weight, smoking, excessive alcohol, certain medications (steroids), low calcium intake, vitamin D deficiency
treatment: Treatment includes bisphosphonates, hormone therapy, denosumab, teriparatide, and romosozumab. Clinical trials explore novel anabolic agents, cathepsin K inhibitors, and combination therapies.
whyParticipate: Osteoporosis clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with convenient dosing schedules and contribute to understanding bone metabolism and fracture prevention.

faq.q: What are osteoporosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Osteoporosis clinical trials test new medications and therapies to increase bone density, reduce fracture risk, and improve bone health.

faq.q: Who can participate in osteoporosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on bone density scores (T-scores), age, fracture history, and prior osteoporosis treatments.

faq.q: How much do osteoporosis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with bone density scans and lab work provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are osteoporosis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are monitored with regular bone density scans and blood work. All trials follow strict safety guidelines.

name: Crohn's Disease
description: Crohn's disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease causing inflammation of the digestive tract. Clinical trials investigate biologic therapies, JAK inhibitors, and novel approaches to induce and maintain remission.
symptoms: Abdominal pain, severe diarrhea, fatigue, weight loss, malnutrition, fever, blood in stool, reduced appetite
riskFactors: Family history, age (usually diagnosed before 30), smoking, certain medications (NSAIDs), environmental factors, ethnic background
treatment: Treatment includes anti-inflammatory drugs, immune system suppressors, biologic therapies (anti-TNF, anti-integrin), antibiotics, and surgery. Clinical trials explore IL-23 inhibitors, oral small molecules, and microbiome-based therapies.
whyParticipate: Crohn's disease clinical trials advance treatment options for patients who do not respond to current therapies and contribute to understanding inflammatory bowel disease mechanisms.

faq.q: What are Crohn's disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Crohn's disease clinical trials test new medications and therapies to reduce intestinal inflammation, maintain remission, and improve quality of life.

faq.q: Who can participate in Crohn's disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on disease location, severity, prior treatments, and whether there are complications like fistulas or strictures.

faq.q: How much do Crohn's disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement. Study medications and monitoring are provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are Crohn's disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including laboratory tests and endoscopic evaluations as needed. Safety is closely overseen by independent committees.

name: Glaucoma
description: Glaucoma is a group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve, often caused by abnormally high eye pressure. Clinical trials investigate new eye drops, laser treatments, surgical techniques, and neuroprotective agents.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic in early stages, gradual loss of peripheral vision, tunnel vision in advanced stages, eye pain, blurred vision, halos around lights
riskFactors: Age, family history, high eye pressure, certain ethnicities (African, Hispanic), thin corneas, diabetes, high blood pressure, eye injury
treatment: Treatment includes prescription eye drops (prostaglandins, beta-blockers, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors), laser trabeculoplasty, and surgical procedures. Clinical trials explore sustained-release drug delivery, gene therapies, and neuroprotective agents.
whyParticipate: Glaucoma clinical trials help develop treatments that can preserve vision and prevent blindness by improving intraocular pressure control and protecting optic nerve health.

faq.q: What are glaucoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Glaucoma clinical trials test new eye drops, laser treatments, surgical devices, and medications to lower eye pressure and preserve vision.

faq.q: Who can participate in glaucoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on glaucoma type (open-angle, angle-closure), disease severity, eye pressure levels, and prior treatments.

faq.q: How much do glaucoma clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with eye exams and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are glaucoma clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular comprehensive eye exams and monitoring. All trials follow strict safety protocols approved by ethics committees.

name: Eczema
description: Eczema (atopic dermatitis) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition causing itchy, red, and irritated skin. Clinical trials investigate topical treatments, systemic medications, and biologic therapies targeting specific immune pathways.
symptoms: Intense itching, red to brownish-gray patches, small raised bumps, thickened scaly skin, raw sensitive skin from scratching, dark-colored patches
riskFactors: Family history, asthma or hay fever, certain immune system variations, skin barrier defects, environmental factors, stress
treatment: Treatment includes moisturizers, topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, phototherapy, systemic immunosuppressants, and biologic therapies (dupilumab, tralokinumab). Clinical trials explore novel JAK inhibitors and microbiome-based approaches.
whyParticipate: Eczema clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with better safety profiles and contribute to understanding the complex mechanisms of atopic dermatitis.

faq.q: What are eczema clinical trials?
faq.a: Eczema clinical trials test new topical treatments, systemic medications, and biologic therapies to reduce inflammation, itching, and improve skin barrier function.

faq.q: Who can participate in eczema clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on eczema severity, body surface area affected, age, and prior treatment history.

faq.q: How much do eczema clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and dermatology exams provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are eczema clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are monitored regularly for skin improvement and potential side effects. All trials have safety oversight.

name: Hepatitis B
description: Hepatitis B is a viral infection causing liver inflammation that can become chronic and lead to cirrhosis or liver cancer. Clinical trials investigate new antiviral therapies, immune-based treatments, and functional cure strategies.
symptoms: Fatigue, jaundice, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, dark urine, joint pain, loss of appetite, fever
riskFactors: Birth to infected mother, unprotected sex, sharing needles, occupational exposure, hemodialysis, travel to endemic areas
treatment: Treatment includes antiviral medications (tenofovir, entecavir) and pegylated interferon. Clinical trials explore novel entry inhibitors, capsid inhibitors, RNA interference therapies, and immunomodulatory approaches toward functional cure.
whyParticipate: Hepatitis B clinical trials are essential for developing finite treatment regimens and potential cures for the hundreds of millions living with chronic hepatitis B worldwide.

faq.q: What are hepatitis B clinical trials?
faq.a: Hepatitis B clinical trials test new antiviral medications and immune-based therapies to improve viral suppression and potentially achieve functional cure.

faq.q: Who can participate in hepatitis B clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on hepatitis B status (chronic vs. resolved), viral load, liver function, and prior treatment history.

faq.q: How much do hepatitis B clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are hepatitis B clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular liver function monitoring and viral load testing. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Seasonal Allergies
description: Seasonal allergies (allergic rhinitis) occur when the immune system overreacts to airborne substances like pollen. Clinical trials investigate new antihistamines, nasal sprays, immunotherapy approaches, and biologic therapies.
symptoms: Sneezing, runny or stuffy nose, itchy eyes, nose or throat, watery eyes, postnasal drip, cough, fatigue from poor sleep
riskFactors: Family history of allergies, personal history of asthma or eczema, exposure to pollen, mold, dust mites, or pet dander
treatment: Treatment includes antihistamines, nasal corticosteroid sprays, decongestants, eye drops, and allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots or sublingual tablets). Clinical trials explore novel biologic therapies targeting allergic pathways and improved immunotherapy formulations.
whyParticipate: Seasonal allergy clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with faster onset and fewer side effects, improving quality of life for millions affected by allergies.

faq.q: What are seasonal allergy clinical trials?
faq.a: Seasonal allergy clinical trials test new medications, nasal sprays, and immunotherapy treatments to reduce allergic reactions and improve symptom control.

faq.q: Who can participate in seasonal allergy clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires confirmed allergic rhinitis with sensitivity to relevant allergens. Studies often exclude those with severe asthma or other conditions.

faq.q: How much do seasonal allergy clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications provided at no cost during allergy season.

faq.q: Are seasonal allergy clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are monitored for allergic reactions and side effects. Trials have safety protocols and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Thyroid Disorders
description: Thyroid disorders include hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and thyroid nodules affecting metabolic regulation throughout the body. Clinical trials investigate new hormone replacement therapies, treatments for autoimmune thyroid conditions, and thyroid cancer management approaches.
symptoms: Fatigue, weight changes, temperature sensitivity, heart rate changes, mood swings, hair loss, goiter, muscle weakness
riskFactors: Family history, female gender, autoimmune conditions, iodine deficiency or excess, radiation exposure, certain medications
treatment: Treatment includes synthetic thyroid hormone for hypothyroidism, antithyroid medications and radioactive iodine for hyperthyroidism, and surgery when indicated. Clinical trials explore targeted therapies for thyroid cancer and immunomodulatory approaches for autoimmune thyroiditis.
whyParticipate: Thyroid disorder clinical trials help develop more precise dosing strategies and novel treatments for conditions affecting millions worldwide.

faq.q: What are thyroid disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Thyroid disorder clinical trials test new medications, hormone formulations, and treatments for hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and thyroid cancer. They aim to improve symptom control and reduce side effects of current therapies.

faq.q: Who can participate in thyroid disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the specific thyroid condition, hormone levels, and prior treatments. Studies may focus on newly diagnosed patients or those who have not responded well to standard therapy.

faq.q: How much do thyroid disorder clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with study-related lab work and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are thyroid disorder clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular thyroid function monitoring and medical oversight. All trials follow strict safety protocols approved by ethics committees.

name: Anemia
description: Anemia is a blood disorder characterized by a deficiency of red blood cells or hemoglobin, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues. Clinical trials investigate new iron formulations, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, and treatments for inherited anemias.
symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, pale skin, shortness of breath, dizziness, cold hands and feet, chest pain, headache, irregular heartbeat
riskFactors: Poor diet (iron, B12, folate deficiency), chronic diseases, heavy menstruation, pregnancy, family history of inherited anemias, certain medications
treatment: Treatment depends on the cause and includes iron supplementation, vitamin B12 injections, folic acid, and blood transfusions for severe cases. Clinical trials explore novel oral iron formulations, gene therapies for sickle cell disease, and targeted treatments for anemia of chronic disease.
whyParticipate: Anemia clinical trials contribute to developing more tolerable and effective treatments that can improve energy levels and quality of life.

faq.q: What are anemia clinical trials?
faq.a: Anemia clinical trials test new iron supplements, vitamin formulations, and medications that stimulate red blood cell production. These studies aim to find better treatments for various types of anemia with fewer gastrointestinal side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in anemia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the type and severity of anemia, underlying causes, and prior treatments. Studies may focus on iron-deficiency anemia, anemia of chronic disease, or inherited anemias.

faq.q: How much do anemia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit plus travel reimbursement for study-related appointments.

faq.q: Are anemia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular blood tests and monitoring throughout the study. All trials have safety oversight and ethics committee approval.

name: Fibromyalgia
description: Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and tender points. Clinical trials investigate new pain medications, nerve modulation therapies, and integrative treatment approaches.
symptoms: Widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, cognitive difficulty (fibro fog), headaches, irritable bowel syndrome, anxiety, depression, morning stiffness
riskFactors: Family history, female gender, trauma or injury, infections, autoimmune conditions, repetitive stress injuries, emotional distress
treatment: Treatment includes pain medications (pregabalin, duloxetine), exercise therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, and sleep management. Clinical trials explore novel neuropathic pain agents, low-dose naltrexone, and non-invasive neuromodulation techniques.
whyParticipate: Fibromyalgia clinical trials are crucial for developing more effective treatments for a condition where many patients have inadequate symptom relief.

faq.q: What are fibromyalgia clinical trials?
faq.a: Fibromyalgia clinical trials test new medications, physical therapies, and neuromodulation approaches to reduce pain and improve daily function. They aim to find treatments that address both pain and associated symptoms like fatigue and sleep problems.

faq.q: Who can participate in fibromyalgia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a confirmed fibromyalgia diagnosis based on standardized criteria. Factors include pain severity, symptom duration, and prior treatment responses.

faq.q: How much do fibromyalgia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $75-$200 per visit plus travel reimbursement for study appointments.

faq.q: Are fibromyalgia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular health monitoring throughout the trial. All studies have safety protocols and participants can withdraw at any time.

name: Gout
description: Gout is a painful form of arthritis caused by uric acid crystal buildup in joints. Clinical trials investigate new urate-lowering therapies, anti-inflammatory agents for flare prevention, and treatments for refractory gout.
symptoms: Intense joint pain (often big toe), swelling, redness, warmth, lingering discomfort, limited range of motion, tophi lumps under skin
riskFactors: High purine diet, alcohol consumption, obesity, male gender, family history, kidney disease, certain medications (diuretics), hypertension
treatment: Treatment includes NSAIDs, colchicine, corticosteroids for acute flares, and urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol or febuxostat. Clinical trials explore novel URAT1 inhibitors, recombinant uricase formulations, and targeted anti-inflammatory agents.
whyParticipate: Gout clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with fewer drug interactions and better long-term uric acid control.

faq.q: What are gout clinical trials?
faq.a: Gout clinical trials test new medications to lower uric acid levels and prevent painful flares. Studies investigate more convenient dosing options and treatments for patients who do not respond to standard therapies.

faq.q: Who can participate in gout clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on serum uric acid levels, flare frequency, presence of tophi, and prior treatment history. Some studies focus on refractory gout.

faq.q: How much do gout clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and lab work provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are gout clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are monitored with regular blood tests and joint assessments. All trials follow strict safety guidelines.

name: Ulcerative Colitis
description: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease causing inflammation and ulcers in the colon and rectum. Clinical trials investigate biologic therapies, small molecule inhibitors, and microbiome-based treatments to induce and maintain remission.
symptoms: Diarrhea with blood or pus, abdominal pain, rectal pain, urgency, fecal incontinence, fatigue, weight loss, fever, anemia
riskFactors: Family history, age (usually 15-30), ethnic background (higher in Caucasians and Ashkenazi Jews), certain environmental factors, immune system abnormalities
treatment: Treatment includes aminosalicylates, corticosteroids, immunomodulators, biologic therapies (anti-TNF, anti-integrin), JAK inhibitors, and colectomy for severe cases. Clinical trials explore S1P receptor modulators, IL-23 inhibitors, and fecal microbiota transplantation.
whyParticipate: Ulcerative colitis clinical trials advance treatment options for achieving steroid-free remission and preventing disease complications.

faq.q: What are ulcerative colitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Ulcerative colitis clinical trials test new medications and therapies to reduce colon inflammation and maintain long-term remission. Studies evaluate both oral and injectable treatments with different mechanisms of action.

faq.q: Who can participate in ulcerative colitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on disease extent and severity, prior treatment response, and whether complications are present. Studies often require endoscopic confirmation of active disease.

faq.q: How much do ulcerative colitis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with colonoscopies and lab work provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are ulcerative colitis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including blood tests and endoscopic evaluations as needed. Independent safety committees oversee all trials.

name: Celiac Disease
description: Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten consumption that damages the small intestine. Clinical trials investigate enzyme therapies, immunomodulatory agents, and vaccine-based approaches to restore gluten tolerance.
symptoms: Diarrhea, bloating, gas, abdominal pain, fatigue, weight loss, anemia, bone density loss, skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis), headaches
riskFactors: Family history, type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease, Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, Caucasian ethnicity, female gender
treatment: The only current treatment is a strict lifelong gluten-free diet. Clinical trials explore oral enzyme supplements that break down gluten, immune tolerance induction therapies, and medications that prevent intestinal damage from gluten exposure.
whyParticipate: Celiac disease clinical trials are essential for developing treatments that allow patients to safely consume gluten, significantly improving quality of life.

faq.q: What are celiac disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Celiac disease clinical trials test enzyme therapies, immune-modulating drugs, and vaccine approaches to protect patients from gluten exposure. These studies aim to develop options beyond the strict gluten-free diet.

faq.q: Who can participate in celiac disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a confirmed celiac diagnosis through blood antibodies and intestinal biopsy. Studies may require patients to maintain a gluten-free diet before enrollment.

faq.q: How much do celiac disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study-related endoscopies and lab work provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are celiac disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants are closely monitored with regular blood tests and symptom tracking. All trials have ethics committee approval and safety oversight.

name: Macular Degeneration
description: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is an eye condition causing central vision loss due to damage to the macula. Clinical trials investigate anti-VEGF therapies, gene therapies, stem cell treatments, and implantable drug delivery systems.
symptoms: Blurred central vision, difficulty reading or recognizing faces, dark spots in central vision, distorted vision (straight lines appear wavy), decreased color intensity
riskFactors: Age (over 50), family history, smoking, obesity, cardiovascular disease, high blood pressure, light eye color, prolonged sun exposure
treatment: Treatment includes anti-VEGF injections, photodynamic therapy, laser photocoagulation, and nutritional supplements for dry AMD. Clinical trials explore gene therapies for wet AMD, complement inhibitors for geographic atrophy, and port delivery systems for sustained drug release.
whyParticipate: Macular degeneration clinical trials are vital for developing treatments that can preserve vision and prevent blindness in the aging population.

faq.q: What are macular degeneration clinical trials?
faq.a: Macular degeneration clinical trials test new eye injections, gene therapies, and sustained-release implants to preserve and potentially restore vision. They aim to reduce treatment burden and improve visual outcomes.

faq.q: Who can participate in macular degeneration clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on AMD type (dry or wet), disease stage, visual acuity, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on early-stage disease while others include advanced cases.

faq.q: How much do macular degeneration clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with comprehensive eye exams and study treatments provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are macular degeneration clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular eye exams and monitoring throughout the study. All trials have safety committees and follow strict ethical guidelines.

name: Endometriosis
description: Endometriosis is a painful condition where uterine-like tissue grows outside the uterus, affecting reproductive organs. Clinical trials investigate new hormonal therapies, pain management approaches, and fertility preservation techniques.
symptoms: Pelvic pain, painful periods, pain during intercourse, heavy menstrual bleeding, infertility, fatigue, gastrointestinal symptoms, back pain
riskFactors: Family history, early menstruation, short menstrual cycles, never having given birth, uterine abnormalities, high estrogen levels
treatment: Treatment includes NSAIDs, hormonal contraceptives, GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, and laparoscopic surgery to remove endometrial lesions. Clinical trials explore novel GnRH antagonists, selective progesterone receptor modulators, and neurokinin receptor antagonists.
whyParticipate: Endometriosis clinical trials help develop treatments that preserve fertility and provide better pain relief for millions of women affected by this condition.

faq.q: What are endometriosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Endometriosis clinical trials test new hormonal medications, pain therapies, and surgical approaches to reduce endometrial growth and relieve pelvic pain. These studies aim to improve symptom control while preserving fertility.

faq.q: Who can participate in endometriosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically requires a confirmed endometriosis diagnosis, often through laparoscopy. Factors include pain severity, disease stage, and fertility goals.

faq.q: How much do endometriosis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $75-$200 per visit with study-related examinations and treatments provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are endometriosis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular medical monitoring throughout the study. All trials have independent safety oversight and ethics committee approval.

name: PCOS
description: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) is a hormonal disorder causing enlarged ovaries with small cysts, affecting fertility and metabolism. Clinical trials investigate insulin-sensitizing agents, hormonal therapies, and lifestyle interventions for symptom management.
symptoms: Irregular periods, excess androgen causing hirsutism and acne, polycystic ovaries, weight gain, hair thinning, infertility, skin darkening
riskFactors: Family history, obesity, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, sedentary lifestyle, genetic predisposition
treatment: Treatment includes birth control pills for cycle regulation, metformin for insulin resistance, anti-androgen medications, and fertility treatments. Clinical trials explore novel insulin sensitizers, GLP-1 agonists for weight management, and targeted hormonal therapies.
whyParticipate: PCOS clinical trials advance treatment options for improving metabolic health, fertility outcomes, and quality of life in affected women.

faq.q: What are PCOS clinical trials?
faq.a: PCOS clinical trials test new medications and lifestyle interventions to regulate periods, reduce androgen levels, and improve fertility. Studies explore treatments targeting insulin resistance and hormonal imbalances.

faq.q: Who can participate in PCOS clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a PCOS diagnosis based on Rotterdam criteria. Factors include age, symptom profile, metabolic status, and fertility goals.

faq.q: How much do PCOS clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with study-related lab work and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are PCOS clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring of hormone levels and metabolic markers. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Bipolar Disorder
description: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder characterized by alternating episodes of mania and depression affecting energy and functioning. Clinical trials investigate mood stabilizers, novel antipsychotics, and personalized treatment approaches based on genetic markers.
symptoms: Mood swings between mania (elevated energy, reduced need for sleep, grandiosity) and depression (sadness, low energy, suicidal thoughts), racing thoughts, poor judgment
riskFactors: Family history, genetic factors, high stress, trauma, substance use, sleep disruption, major life changes
treatment: Treatment includes mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate), atypical antipsychotics, antidepressants under careful monitoring, and psychotherapy. Clinical trials explore targeted therapies for specific bipolar subtypes and novel mechanisms for mood stabilization.
whyParticipate: Bipolar disorder clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with fewer side effects, addressing the high need for improved mood stabilization options.

faq.q: What are bipolar disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Bipolar disorder clinical trials test new mood stabilizers, antipsychotics, and psychotherapeutic approaches to manage manic and depressive episodes. Studies aim to find treatments that provide better symptom control with fewer metabolic side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in bipolar disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a bipolar disorder diagnosis (Type I or II) based on standardized psychiatric criteria. Factors include mood episode frequency, severity, and prior treatment history.

faq.q: How much do bipolar disorder clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with regular psychiatric monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are bipolar disorder clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close psychiatric monitoring throughout the study. Safety protocols are in place and participants can access rescue treatment if needed.

name: Schizophrenia
description: Schizophrenia is a severe mental disorder affecting thoughts, emotions, and behavior, characterized by psychosis and cognitive impairment. Clinical trials investigate novel antipsychotics, cognitive remediation therapies, and early intervention strategies.
symptoms: Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking, reduced emotional expression, social withdrawal, cognitive deficits, lack of motivation, unusual movements
riskFactors: Family history, genetic factors, birth complications, prenatal infection or malnutrition, stress, substance use (especially cannabis), older paternal age
treatment: Treatment includes antipsychotic medications, psychosocial interventions, cognitive behavioral therapy, coordinated specialty care, and vocational support. Clinical trials explore trace amine-associated receptor agonists, muscarinic receptor modulators, and cognitive training interventions.
whyParticipate: Schizophrenia clinical trials are essential for developing treatments that address negative symptoms and cognitive deficits with better tolerability.

faq.q: What are schizophrenia clinical trials?
faq.a: Schizophrenia clinical trials test new antipsychotic medications and therapeutic interventions to reduce symptoms and improve daily functioning. Studies explore novel drug mechanisms that may offer better efficacy with fewer side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in schizophrenia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a schizophrenia diagnosis confirmed by psychiatric evaluation. Factors include symptom severity, illness duration, and prior treatment responses.

faq.q: How much do schizophrenia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and psychiatric monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are schizophrenia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular psychiatric assessments and medical monitoring. All trials have safety oversight and ethical review board approval.

name: Substance Use Disorder
description: Substance Use Disorder is a condition involving problematic use of alcohol, opioids, stimulants, or other substances despite negative consequences. Clinical trials investigate medication-assisted treatments, behavioral interventions, and relapse prevention strategies.
symptoms: Cravings, loss of control over use, withdrawal symptoms, tolerance, neglecting responsibilities, continued use despite problems, risky behaviors
riskFactors: Family history, early substance use, mental health conditions, trauma, peer pressure, poverty, availability of substances, genetic predisposition
treatment: Treatment includes detoxification, medication-assisted treatment (methadone, buprenorphine, naltrexone), counseling, cognitive behavioral therapy, and support groups. Clinical trials explore novel anti-craving medications, vaccine-based approaches, and digital therapeutic interventions.
whyParticipate: Substance use disorder clinical trials help develop more effective treatments for addiction, addressing the opioid epidemic and improving recovery outcomes.

faq.q: What are substance use disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Substance use disorder clinical trials test new medications, therapies, and digital tools to reduce cravings, prevent relapse, and support recovery. Studies focus on both pharmacological and behavioral approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in substance use disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on the specific substance (alcohol, opioids, stimulants), severity of use, treatment history, and motivation for recovery. Some studies require a period of abstinence.

faq.q: How much do substance use disorder clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with counseling and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are substance use disorder clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular medical monitoring and access to clinical support. All trials have safety protocols and ethics committee oversight.

name: Lupus
description: Lupus (systemic lupus erythematosus) is a chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks healthy tissues affecting multiple organs. Clinical trials investigate biologic therapies, targeted immunomodulators, and approaches to reduce disease flares.
symptoms: Butterfly-shaped facial rash, joint pain and swelling, fatigue, fever, chest pain, kidney problems, photosensitivity, hair loss, mouth ulcers
riskFactors: Female gender, age (15-45), family history, certain ethnicities (African American, Hispanic, Asian), certain medications, Epstein-Barr virus, sunlight exposure
treatment: Treatment includes NSAIDs, antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine), corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, and biologic therapies (belimumab). Clinical trials explore novel B-cell targeting agents, interferon pathway inhibitors, and CAR T-cell therapy for lupus.
whyParticipate: Lupus clinical trials advance treatment options for this complex autoimmune disease with limited therapeutic options, especially for severe organ involvement.
