faq.q: What are lupus clinical trials?
faq.a: Lupus clinical trials test new medications and immunotherapies to reduce autoimmune activity and prevent organ damage. Studies aim to reduce disease flares and steroid dependence.

faq.q: Who can participate in lupus clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on lupus type and severity, organ involvement, disease activity level, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on lupus nephritis or cutaneous lupus.

faq.q: How much do lupus clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are lupus clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including blood tests and organ function assessments. All trials have independent safety oversight.

name: Osteoarthritis
description: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease where cartilage breaks down over time, causing pain and stiffness. Clinical trials investigate disease-modifying osteoarthritis drugs, regenerative therapies, and pain management innovations.
symptoms: Joint pain during movement, morning stiffness, tenderness, loss of flexibility, grating sensation, bone spurs, joint swelling
riskFactors: Age, joint injury or overuse, obesity, family history, gender (more common in women), bone deformities, certain metabolic conditions
treatment: Treatment includes NSAIDs, acetaminophen, corticosteroid injections, hyaluronic acid injections, physical therapy, and joint replacement surgery. Clinical trials explore nerve growth factor inhibitors, stem cell therapies, and novel anti-inflammatory agents.
whyParticipate: Osteoarthritis clinical trials help develop treatments that can modify disease progression rather than just manage symptoms, addressing a major unmet medical need.

faq.q: What are osteoarthritis clinical trials?
faq.a: Osteoarthritis clinical trials test new medications, injections, and regenerative therapies to reduce joint pain and potentially slow cartilage breakdown. Studies explore both symptomatic treatments and disease-modifying approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in osteoarthritis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on which joints are affected (knee, hip, hand), disease severity on x-ray, pain levels, and prior treatments.

faq.q: How much do osteoarthritis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with study-related imaging and evaluations provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are osteoarthritis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular medical monitoring including joint assessments. All trials follow strict safety guidelines.

name: Stroke
description: A stroke occurs when blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted, causing brain cell death and neurological deficits. Clinical trials investigate acute interventions, neuroprotective agents, and rehabilitation strategies to improve recovery.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness on one side, confusion, trouble speaking, vision problems, dizziness, severe headache with no known cause, difficulty walking
riskFactors: High blood pressure, smoking, diabetes, high cholesterol, obesity, atrial fibrillation, age, family history, sedentary lifestyle
treatment: Acute treatment includes clot-dissolving medications (tPA), mechanical thrombectomy, and supportive care. Rehabilitation involves physical, occupational, and speech therapy. Clinical trials explore neuroprotective drugs, stem cell therapies, and brain-computer interfaces for recovery.
whyParticipate: Stroke clinical trials are critical for developing treatments that can minimize brain damage and improve functional recovery after stroke.

faq.q: What are stroke clinical trials?
faq.a: Stroke clinical trials test new clot-dissolving medications, neuroprotective agents, and rehabilitation therapies to improve outcomes after stroke. Studies include both acute intervention and long-term recovery approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in stroke clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on stroke type (ischemic or hemorrhagic), time since stroke onset, severity of deficits, and overall health. Some studies focus on acute treatment within hours of symptoms.

faq.q: How much do stroke clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with rehabilitation services and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are stroke clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring throughout the study. All trials have safety protocols and ethics committee approval.

name: Atrial Fibrillation
description: Atrial fibrillation is an irregular and often rapid heart rhythm that increases stroke risk. Clinical trials investigate new anticoagulants, rhythm control medications, catheter ablation techniques, and left atrial appendage closure devices.
symptoms: Heart palpitations, shortness of breath, fatigue, dizziness, chest pain, fainting, weakness, reduced exercise tolerance
riskFactors: Age, high blood pressure, heart disease, obesity, diabetes, sleep apnea, excessive alcohol consumption, hyperthyroidism, family history
treatment: Treatment includes anticoagulants to prevent stroke, rate control medications (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers), rhythm control medications (antiarrhythmics), cardioversion, and catheter ablation. Clinical trials explore pulsed field ablation, novel anticoagulants, and hybrid ablation procedures.
whyParticipate: Atrial fibrillation clinical trials advance treatment options for maintaining normal heart rhythm and reducing stroke risk with improved safety profiles.

faq.q: What are atrial fibrillation clinical trials?
faq.a: Atrial fibrillation clinical trials test new medications, ablation technologies, and blood thinners to control heart rhythm and prevent stroke. Studies aim to improve treatment efficacy and safety.

faq.q: Who can participate in atrial fibrillation clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on AF type (paroxysmal, persistent, permanent), symptom frequency, prior treatments, and stroke risk factors.

faq.q: How much do atrial fibrillation clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $75-$200 per visit with cardiac monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are atrial fibrillation clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular cardiac monitoring including ECGs and Holter monitoring. All trials have independent safety oversight.

name: Epilepsy
description: Epilepsy is a neurological disorder characterized by recurrent seizures due to abnormal brain activity. Clinical trials investigate new antiseizure medications, neuromodulation devices, dietary therapies, and surgical approaches.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures ranging from brief staring spells to full-body convulsions, confusion after seizures, temporary confusion, uncontrollable jerking movements, loss of consciousness
riskFactors: Family history, head injury, stroke, brain infection, dementia, age (young children and older adults), prenatal injury, developmental disorders
treatment: Treatment includes antiseizure medications (AEDs), vagus nerve stimulation, responsive neurostimulation, ketogenic diet, and surgery to remove seizure foci. Clinical trials explore novel GABAergic agents, potassium channel openers, and closed-loop stimulation systems.
whyParticipate: Epilepsy clinical trials help develop treatments for drug-resistant epilepsy and improve seizure control with fewer cognitive side effects.

faq.q: What are epilepsy clinical trials?
faq.a: Epilepsy clinical trials test new anticonvulsant medications, neurostimulation devices, and dietary interventions to reduce seizure frequency. Studies include both pediatric and adult populations with various seizure types.

faq.q: Who can participate in epilepsy clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on seizure type, frequency, epilepsy syndrome, age of onset, and prior treatment responses. Some studies focus on drug-resistant epilepsy.

faq.q: How much do epilepsy clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with study medications and EEG monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are epilepsy clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close neurological monitoring throughout the trial. Rescue seizure medications are available and safety protocols are strictly followed.

name: Dementia
description: Dementia is a decline in cognitive function affecting memory, thinking, and daily activities beyond normal aging. Clinical trials investigate disease-modifying therapies, cognitive training interventions, and treatments for behavioral symptoms.
symptoms: Memory loss, difficulty communicating, poor judgment, confusion, personality changes, trouble with complex tasks, repetition, wandering, loss of spatial awareness
riskFactors: Age, family history, genetic factors (APOE-e4), cardiovascular risk factors, head injury, limited education, social isolation, hearing loss
treatment: Treatment includes cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine for symptom management, along with behavioral interventions and caregiver support. Clinical trials explore anti-amyloid and anti-tau therapies, neuroprotective agents, and lifestyle-based prevention approaches.
whyParticipate: Dementia clinical trials are essential for finding treatments that can slow cognitive decline and improve quality of life for millions affected by age-related cognitive impairment.

faq.q: What are dementia clinical trials?
faq.a: Dementia clinical trials test new medications and interventions to slow cognitive decline and manage behavioral symptoms. Studies include both pharmacological and lifestyle-based approaches to brain health.

faq.q: Who can participate in dementia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on dementia type and stage, cognitive assessment scores, and overall health. Some studies include people with early symptoms while others focus on prevention.

faq.q: How much do dementia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with cognitive assessments and travel reimbursement provided.

faq.q: Are dementia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular cognitive and medical monitoring. Caregiver support is often available and all trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Narcolepsy
description: Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder causing overwhelming daytime drowsiness and sudden sleep attacks. Clinical trials investigate novel wake-promoting agents, orexin receptor agonists, and treatments for cataplexy and other symptoms.
symptoms: Excessive daytime sleepiness, sudden loss of muscle tone (cataplexy), sleep paralysis, hallucinations upon falling asleep, disrupted nighttime sleep, automatic behaviors
riskFactors: Family history, autoimmune conditions, certain infections (H1N1), head injury, age (usually 10-30), genetic markers (HLA-DQB1*0602)
treatment: Treatment includes stimulants (modafinil, armodafinil), sodium oxybate, antidepressants for cataplexy, and scheduled naps. Clinical trials explore novel orexin receptor agonists, histamine H3 receptor inverse agonists, and immunotherapy approaches.
whyParticipate: Narcolepsy clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with better tolerability for managing sleep attacks and cataplexy symptoms.

faq.q: What are narcolepsy clinical trials?
faq.a: Narcolepsy clinical trials test new medications to promote wakefulness, reduce cataplexy attacks, and improve nighttime sleep quality. Studies explore novel mechanisms including orexin replacement therapies.

faq.q: Who can participate in narcolepsy clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a narcolepsy diagnosis confirmed by sleep studies including multiple sleep latency testing. Factors include cataplexy presence and current medication regimen.

faq.q: How much do narcolepsy clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $75-$200 per visit with sleep studies and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are narcolepsy clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular sleep monitoring and medical oversight. All trials follow strict safety protocols with ethics committee approval.

name: Rosacea
description: Rosacea is a chronic skin condition causing facial redness, visible blood vessels, and sometimes small red bumps. Clinical trials investigate topical treatments, oral medications, laser therapies, and novel approaches to control inflammation.
symptoms: Facial redness and flushing, visible blood vessels (telangiectasias), red bumps and pus-filled lesions, eye irritation (ocular rosacea), skin thickening (especially nose), burning or stinging
riskFactors: Fair skin, family history, age (30-50), female gender, sun exposure, certain triggers (spicy foods, alcohol, temperature extremes), Demodex mites
treatment: Treatment includes topical medications (metronidazole, ivermectin, azelaic acid), oral antibiotics (doxycycline), laser therapy, and trigger avoidance. Clinical trials explore newer topical formulations, targeted anti-inflammatory agents, and improved laser technologies.
whyParticipate: Rosacea clinical trials help develop more effective treatments that can reduce facial redness and prevent disease progression with fewer side effects.

faq.q: What are rosacea clinical trials?
faq.a: Rosacea clinical trials test new topical creams, oral medications, and laser treatments to reduce facial redness and inflammation. Studies aim to find treatments that target the underlying causes of rosacea.

faq.q: Who can participate in rosacea clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on rosacea subtype (erythematotelangiectatic, papulopustular, etc.), severity, and prior treatments. Studies often exclude those with severe ocular rosacea.

faq.q: How much do rosacea clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and dermatology exams provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are rosacea clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular skin assessments and monitoring. All trials follow safety guidelines approved by ethics committees.

name: Vitiligo
description: Vitiligo is an autoimmune skin condition causing loss of pigment-producing cells leading to white patches on the skin. Clinical trials investigate topical JAK inhibitors, phototherapy combinations, and repigmentation therapies.
symptoms: White patches of skin on various body areas, premature graying of hair, loss of color in mucous membranes, sensitivity to sunburn on depigmented areas
riskFactors: Family history, autoimmune conditions (thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes), certain genetic variants, stress, sunburn, chemical exposure
treatment: Treatment includes topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, phototherapy (narrowband UVB), excimer laser, and JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib). Clinical trials explore melanocyte transplantation, topical ruxolitinib combinations, and immune-modulating agents.
whyParticipate: Vitiligo clinical trials contribute to developing more effective repigmentation treatments and understanding the autoimmune mechanisms underlying pigment loss.

faq.q: What are vitiligo clinical trials?
faq.a: Vitiligo clinical trials test new topical medications, light therapies, and surgical techniques to restore skin pigmentation. Studies explore JAK inhibitors and other targeted immunomodulators.

faq.q: Who can participate in vitiligo clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on vitiligo extent and location, disease activity, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on facial or hand vitiligo which is most impactful.

faq.q: How much do vitiligo clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with phototherapy and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are vitiligo clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular skin examinations and monitoring. All trials have safety oversight and follow strict ethical guidelines.

name: Pneumonia
description: Pneumonia is a lung infection causing inflammation of air sacs that fill with fluid or pus. Clinical trials investigate new antibiotics, vaccine strategies, and adjunctive therapies to improve outcomes and combat antimicrobial resistance.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm, fever, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain when breathing deeply, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, confusion (especially in elderly)
riskFactors: Age (very young or elderly), chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, diabetes), smoking, immunocompromised status, recent viral infection, hospitalization, aspiration
treatment: Treatment includes antibiotics for bacterial pneumonia, antiviral medications for viral pneumonia, oxygen therapy, and supportive care. Clinical trials explore novel antibiotics for resistant organisms, monoclonal antibodies against respiratory pathogens, and immune-modulating therapies.
whyParticipate: Pneumonia clinical trials are crucial for developing new treatments against drug-resistant bacteria and improving outcomes in vulnerable populations.

faq.q: What are pneumonia clinical trials?
faq.a: Pneumonia clinical trials test new antibiotics, antiviral medications, and immune therapies to treat lung infections more effectively. Studies address the growing challenge of antibiotic-resistant pneumonia.

faq.q: Who can participate in pneumonia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on pneumonia type (community-acquired, hospital-acquired), severity, causative organism, and underlying health conditions.

faq.q: How much do pneumonia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost during hospitalization.

faq.q: Are pneumonia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring throughout treatment. All trials have safety protocols and participants can receive standard care if needed.

name: Tuberculosis
description: Tuberculosis (TB) is a serious bacterial infection primarily affecting the lungs but can spread to other organs. Clinical trials investigate shorter treatment regimens, drug-resistant TB therapies, and improved vaccine candidates.
symptoms: Persistent cough (lasting 3+ weeks), coughing up blood, chest pain, fever, night sweats, weight loss, fatigue, loss of appetite
riskFactors: Close contact with TB patients, HIV infection, immunocompromised status, living or traveling to endemic areas, poverty, malnutrition, homelessness, incarceration
treatment: Treatment involves a multi-month regimen of antibiotics including rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Clinical trials explore shorter all-oral regimens, novel antibiotics for multidrug-resistant TB, and host-directed therapies.
whyParticipate: Tuberculosis clinical trials help develop shorter, more tolerable treatment regimens to improve completion rates and combat drug-resistant TB globally.

faq.q: What are tuberculosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Tuberculosis clinical trials test new antibiotic combinations and shorter treatment regimens to improve cure rates and reduce drug resistance. Studies include both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant TB.

faq.q: Who can participate in tuberculosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on TB type (pulmonary, extrapulmonary), drug susceptibility, treatment history, and HIV status. Some studies focus on latent TB infection.

faq.q: How much do tuberculosis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are tuberculosis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including sputum tests and chest imaging. All trials have safety oversight and ethics approval.

name: Sinusitis
description: Sinusitis is inflammation of sinus cavities causing facial pain, congestion, and difficulty breathing. Clinical trials investigate new antibiotics, corticosteroid formulations, biologic therapies for chronic sinusitis, and minimally invasive surgical techniques.
symptoms: Facial pressure and pain, nasal congestion, thick nasal discharge, reduced sense of smell, headache, cough, fever, bad breath, tooth pain, fatigue
riskFactors: Seasonal allergies, nasal polyps, asthma, smoking, dental infections, deviated septum, immune deficiency, frequent respiratory infections
treatment: Treatment includes saline irrigation, nasal corticosteroid sprays, decongestants, antibiotics for bacterial infection, and surgery for chronic cases. Clinical trials explore biologic therapies (dupilumab, omalizumab) for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and novel drug-eluting sinus stents.
whyParticipate: Sinusitis clinical trials help develop more targeted treatments that reduce the need for surgery and improve long-term symptom control.

faq.q: What are sinusitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Sinusitis clinical trials test new medications, nasal sprays, and sinus implants to reduce inflammation and improve sinus drainage. Studies include both acute and chronic sinusitis treatments.

faq.q: Who can participate in sinusitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on sinusitis type (acute or chronic), severity, presence of nasal polyps, and prior treatments including surgery history.

faq.q: How much do sinusitis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with sinus imaging and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are sinusitis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including nasal endoscopy as needed. All trials follow safety protocols with ethics committee approval.

name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
description: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) is a non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland causing urinary symptoms in older men. Clinical trials investigate new alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, minimally invasive procedures, and combination therapies.
symptoms: Frequent urination, urgency, weak urine stream, difficulty starting urination, dribbling after urination, nocturia (nighttime urination), straining to urinate
riskFactors: Age (over 40), family history, obesity, lack of exercise, diabetes, heart disease, erectile dysfunction
treatment: Treatment includes alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride), and minimally invasive procedures (TURP, UroLift, Rezum). Clinical trials explore newer water vapor therapy techniques, implantable devices, and combination medical therapies.
whyParticipate: BPH clinical trials help develop less invasive treatments with fewer side effects and better preservation of sexual function.

faq.q: What are BPH clinical trials?
faq.a: BPH clinical trials test new medications, minimally invasive procedures, and implantable devices to relieve urinary obstruction and improve urinary flow. Studies aim to find treatments with fewer sexual side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in BPH clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on symptom severity (IPSS score), prostate size, urinary flow rate, and prior treatments. Studies often exclude those with prostate cancer.

faq.q: How much do BPH clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with urological exams and study procedures provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are BPH clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular urological monitoring including urinary flow measurements. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Erectile Dysfunction
description: Erectile dysfunction (ED) is the inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual activity. Clinical trials investigate new oral medications, topical therapies, shockwave therapy, and regenerative approaches including stem cell treatments.
symptoms: Difficulty getting an erection, difficulty maintaining an erection, reduced sexual desire, premature or delayed ejaculation, relationship stress
riskFactors: Age, diabetes, heart disease, high blood pressure, obesity, smoking, alcohol use, medications, psychological factors, sedentary lifestyle
treatment: Treatment includes oral PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), vacuum erection devices, penile injections, and penile implants. Clinical trials explore novel PDE5 inhibitor formulations, low-intensity shockwave therapy, platelet-rich plasma injections, and gene therapy approaches.
whyParticipate: Erectile dysfunction clinical trials advance treatment options for men who do not respond to current medications and address underlying vascular causes.

faq.q: What are erectile dysfunction clinical trials?
faq.a: Erectile dysfunction clinical trials test new oral medications, topical gels, and non-invasive therapies to improve erectile function. Studies explore regenerative approaches that may address the underlying causes of ED.

faq.q: Who can participate in erectile dysfunction clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on ED severity, duration, underlying causes, and prior treatment responses. Studies may exclude men taking nitrates for heart conditions.

faq.q: How much do erectile dysfunction clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are erectile dysfunction clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular health monitoring including cardiovascular assessments. All trials have safety oversight and ethics approval.

name: Infertility
description: Infertility is the inability to conceive after one year of regular unprotected intercourse, affecting both men and women. Clinical trials investigate new assisted reproductive technologies, fertility preservation methods, and treatments for underlying causes.
symptoms: Difficulty conceiving, irregular menstrual cycles, hormonal imbalances, ovulation disorders, low sperm count or motility, erectile dysfunction, age-related fertility decline
riskFactors: Age (especially over 35), endometriosis, PCOS, tubal blockage, low sperm count, smoking, obesity, stress, sexually transmitted infections
treatment: Treatment includes fertility medications (clomiphene, gonadotropins), intrauterine insemination (IUI), in vitro fertilization (IVF), intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), and donor egg or sperm. Clinical trials explore AI-enhanced embryo selection, time-lapse imaging, and endometrial receptivity testing.
whyParticipate: Infertility clinical trials help improve success rates of fertility treatments and develop new options for those who have not succeeded with current approaches.

faq.q: What are infertility clinical trials?
faq.a: Infertility clinical trials test new fertility medications, assisted reproductive technologies, and diagnostic tools to improve pregnancy rates. Studies explore both male and female fertility factors.

faq.q: Who can participate in infertility clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on age, cause of infertility, previous fertility treatments, and partner's fertility status. Some studies focus on specific conditions like PCOS or unexplained infertility.

faq.q: How much do infertility clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with fertility treatments and monitoring provided at reduced or no cost.

faq.q: Are infertility clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring throughout fertility treatment cycles. All trials have safety protocols and ethics committee oversight.

name: Menopause
description: Menopause is the natural cessation of menstruation marking the end of reproductive years, often accompanied by bothersome symptoms. Clinical trials investigate hormone therapy alternatives, non-hormonal treatments for hot flashes, and interventions for bone health and cognitive changes.
symptoms: Hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, sleep disturbances, mood changes, weight gain, thinning hair, dry skin, joint pain, decreased libido
riskFactors: Age (typically 45-55), family history, smoking, chemotherapy or radiation, hysterectomy with oophorectomy, premature ovarian failure
treatment: Treatment includes hormone replacement therapy (estrogen, progesterone), non-hormonal medications (SSRIs, gabapentin) for hot flashes, vaginal moisturizers, and lifestyle modifications. Clinical trials explore neurokinin receptor antagonists for hot flashes, selective estrogen receptor modulators, and botanical therapies.
whyParticipate: Menopause clinical trials help develop safer and more effective options for managing menopausal symptoms and reducing long-term health risks.

faq.q: What are menopause clinical trials?
faq.a: Menopause clinical trials test new hormone therapies, non-hormonal treatments for hot flashes, and interventions for bone density preservation. Studies explore both pharmaceutical and lifestyle approaches to symptom management.

faq.q: Who can participate in menopause clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility typically includes perimenopausal or postmenopausal women. Factors include symptom severity, time since last period, and health history including breast cancer risk.

faq.q: How much do menopause clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with bone density scans and hormone level monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are menopause clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular health monitoring including breast exams and bone density assessments. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Cataracts
description: Cataracts are clouding of the eye's lens leading to vision impairment, commonly associated with aging. Clinical trials investigate advanced intraocular lens technologies, surgical techniques, and pharmacological approaches to prevent or delay cataract formation.
symptoms: Blurred or cloudy vision, difficulty with night vision, sensitivity to light and glare, halos around lights, fading colors, double vision in one eye, frequent prescription changes
riskFactors: Age, family history, diabetes, smoking, prolonged sun exposure, obesity, high blood pressure, previous eye injury or surgery, prolonged corticosteroid use
treatment: Treatment is surgical removal of the cloudy lens and replacement with an artificial intraocular lens (IOL). Clinical trials explore multifocal and accommodating IOLs, femtosecond laser-assisted surgery, and eye drops that may reverse or prevent cataract formation.
whyParticipate: Cataract clinical trials advance surgical outcomes and vision quality through improved lens technology and less invasive techniques.

faq.q: What are cataract clinical trials?
faq.a: Cataract clinical trials test new intraocular lens designs, surgical techniques, and potential pharmacological treatments to prevent or slow cataract progression. Studies aim to improve visual outcomes and reduce dependence on glasses after surgery.

faq.q: Who can participate in cataract clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on cataract severity, visual acuity, age, and overall eye health. Some studies evaluate specific lens types for astigmatism or presbyopia correction.

faq.q: How much do cataract clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with comprehensive eye exams and surgery provided at reduced or no cost.

faq.q: Are cataract clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive thorough pre- and post-operative monitoring. All surgical trials follow strict safety protocols with experienced surgeons.

name: Sickle Cell Disease
description: Sickle cell disease is an inherited blood disorder where red blood cells become crescent-shaped, causing severe pain and complications. Clinical trials investigate gene therapies, disease-modifying agents, and treatments for acute complications like vaso-occlusive crises.
symptoms: Severe pain episodes (crises), anemia, fatigue, swelling of hands and feet, frequent infections, delayed growth, vision problems, jaundice, stroke risk
riskFactors: Family history, African ancestry, Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, or Indian ancestry, inheritance of two sickle cell genes (HbSS)
treatment: Treatment includes hydroxyurea to reduce crisis frequency, blood transfusions, pain management, and bone marrow transplant. Clinical trials explore CRISPR-based gene editing, gene addition therapies, and novel anti-sickling agents including voxelotor and crizanlizumab.
whyParticipate: Sickle cell disease clinical trials are transforming treatment with gene therapies and disease-modifying agents that offer potential cures.

faq.q: What are sickle cell disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Sickle cell disease clinical trials test gene therapies, disease-modifying medications, and treatments for acute pain crises. Studies are exploring potentially curative approaches using CRISPR and other gene editing technologies.

faq.q: Who can participate in sickle cell disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on genotype (HbSS, HbSC, etc.), crisis frequency, disease complications, and prior treatments. Gene therapy studies often require certain age ranges.

faq.q: How much do sickle cell disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $75-$200 per visit with comprehensive medical monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are sickle cell disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring throughout the study. All trials have independent safety committees and ethical oversight.

name: Hemophilia
description: Hemophilia is a rare bleeding disorder where blood does not clot properly due to deficient clotting factors. Clinical trials investigate gene therapies, extended half-life factor concentrates, and novel non-factor therapies for bleeding prevention.
symptoms: Excessive bleeding from cuts or injuries, large or deep bruises, joint pain and swelling, unexplained nosebleeds, blood in urine or stool, prolonged bleeding after procedures
riskFactors: Family history, male gender (X-linked inheritance), genetic mutation in factor VIII or IX genes, family history of bleeding disorders
treatment: Treatment includes replacement clotting factor concentrates (factor VIII for hemophilia A, factor IX for hemophilia B), desmopressin, and antifibrinolytic medications. Clinical trials explore AAV-based gene therapies, bispecific antibodies (emicizumab), and RNA interference therapies.
whyParticipate: Hemophilia clinical trials are pioneering gene therapy approaches that can provide long-term or permanent correction of the clotting defect.

faq.q: What are hemophilia clinical trials?
faq.a: Hemophilia clinical trials test gene therapies, long-acting clotting factor concentrates, and novel non-factor medications to prevent bleeding episodes. Studies aim to reduce infusion frequency and improve quality of life.

faq.q: Who can participate in hemophilia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on hemophilia type (A or B), severity (factor level), inhibitor status, and prior treatment history. Gene therapy studies have specific age requirements.

faq.q: How much do hemophilia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with factor levels and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are hemophilia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular hematological monitoring including factor levels and inhibitor screening. All trials have strict safety oversight.

name: Cystic Fibrosis
description: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder causing thick mucus buildup in the lungs, pancreas, and other organs. Clinical trials investigate CFTR modulator therapies, gene editing approaches, and treatments for pulmonary exacerbations and complications.
symptoms: Persistent cough with thick mucus, wheezing, frequent lung infections, poor growth despite good appetite, salty-tasting skin, clubbed fingers, male infertility
riskFactors: Family history, Caucasian ethnicity, genetic carrier status (CFTR gene mutations), having two copies of the defective gene
treatment: Treatment includes CFTR modulators (ivacaftor, lumacaftor, tezacaftor, elexacaftor), airway clearance techniques, inhaled medications, pancreatic enzymes, and nutritional support. Clinical trials explore next-generation CFTR correctors, mRNA therapies, and CRISPR-based gene repair.
whyParticipate: Cystic fibrosis clinical trials have transformed the treatment landscape with CFTR modulators and continue pursuing curative gene-based approaches.

faq.q: What are cystic fibrosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Cystic fibrosis clinical trials test CFTR modulator therapies, gene editing treatments, and medications to reduce lung infections and improve respiratory function. Studies are pursuing both symptom management and curative approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in cystic fibrosis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on CFTR gene mutations, lung function (FEV1), age, and prior CFTR modulator use. Some studies focus on specific mutation classes.

faq.q: How much do cystic fibrosis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with pulmonary function tests and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are cystic fibrosis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular pulmonary monitoring and medical oversight. All trials follow strict safety protocols with ethics committee approval.

name: Scleroderma
description: Scleroderma is a chronic autoimmune disease causing hardening and tightening of the skin and connective tissues. Clinical trials investigate immunosuppressive therapies, anti-fibrotic agents, and treatments for organ-specific complications like pulmonary fibrosis.
symptoms: Hardening and thickening of skin, Raynaud's phenomenon (fingers turning white/blue in cold), heartburn, joint pain, shortness of breath, calcium deposits in skin, telangiectasias
riskFactors: Female gender, age (30-50), family history, certain ethnic backgrounds (African American, Native American), environmental exposures (silica dust), certain medications
treatment: Treatment includes immunosuppressants (methotrexate, mycophenolate), vasodilators for Raynaud's, proton pump inhibitors for reflux, and medications for pulmonary hypertension. Clinical trials explore tyrosine kinase inhibitors (nintedanib), endothelin receptor antagonists, and autologous stem cell transplantation.
whyParticipate: Scleroderma clinical trials advance treatment options for this rare disease with limited therapeutic options and significant organ involvement.

faq.q: What are scleroderma clinical trials?
faq.a: Scleroderma clinical trials test new immunosuppressive and anti-fibrotic medications to slow disease progression and manage organ complications. Studies focus on both skin and internal organ involvement.

faq.q: Who can participate in scleroderma clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on scleroderma type (limited or diffuse), disease duration, organ involvement, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on pulmonary complications.

faq.q: How much do scleroderma clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with comprehensive monitoring and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are scleroderma clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including skin scores and organ function tests. All trials have independent safety oversight.

name: Sjögren's Syndrome
description: Sjögren's syndrome is an autoimmune disorder characterized by dry eyes and dry mouth due to immune attack on moisture-producing glands. Clinical trials investigate immunomodulatory therapies, biologic agents, and treatments for systemic manifestations.
symptoms: Dry eyes (gritty sensation), dry mouth (difficulty swallowing), joint pain, fatigue, swollen salivary glands, dry skin, vaginal dryness, persistent cough
riskFactors: Female gender, age (40-60), family history, other autoimmune conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis), certain genetic markers
treatment: Treatment includes artificial tears and saliva substitutes, prescription medications (pilocarpine, cevimeline) to increase secretions, hydroxychloroquine, and immunosuppressants. Clinical trials explore B-cell targeting therapies (rituximab, belimumab), T-cell co-stimulation blockers, and novel anti-inflammatory agents.
whyParticipate: Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials help develop treatments that address both dryness symptoms and systemic complications of this autoimmune condition.

faq.q: What are Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials?
faq.a: Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials test new medications and biologic therapies to reduce gland inflammation and increase saliva and tear production. Studies also address systemic symptoms like fatigue and joint pain.

faq.q: Who can participate in Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility requires a confirmed Sjögren's diagnosis through autoantibodies and salivary gland biopsy. Factors include symptom severity and presence of systemic involvement.

faq.q: How much do Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with study medications and monitoring provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are Sjögren's syndrome clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including eye exams and laboratory tests. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Diverticulitis
description: Diverticulitis is inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) in the digestive tract wall. Clinical trials investigate antibiotic regimens, dietary interventions, probiotic therapies, and minimally invasive surgical approaches.
symptoms: Sudden severe abdominal pain (usually left side), fever, nausea and vomiting, constipation or diarrhea, abdominal tenderness, bloating, rectal bleeding
riskFactors: Age (over 40), obesity, smoking, low-fiber diet, lack of exercise, certain medications (NSAIDs, steroids), collagen vascular disorders
treatment: Treatment includes oral or intravenous antibiotics, liquid diet to rest the colon, and surgery for complicated cases (abscess, perforation). Clinical trials explore antibiotic-sparing strategies, gut microbiome modulation, and elective laparoscopic colectomy techniques.
whyParticipate: Diverticulitis clinical trials advance treatment approaches that reduce hospitalization and prevent recurrent episodes through targeted interventions.

faq.q: What are diverticulitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Diverticulitis clinical trials test new antibiotic regimens, dietary approaches, and surgical techniques to treat colon inflammation and prevent recurrence. Studies explore microbiome-based therapies and less invasive surgical options.

faq.q: Who can participate in diverticulitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on diverticulitis severity, number of episodes, presence of complications, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on uncomplicated diverticulitis for antibiotic-sparing approaches.

faq.q: How much do diverticulitis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with colonoscopy and imaging provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are diverticulitis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular medical monitoring including imaging when needed. All trials have safety oversight and ethics committee approval.

name: Pancreatitis
description: Pancreatitis is inflammation of the pancreas causing severe abdominal pain and digestive complications. Clinical trials investigate new treatments for acute pancreatitis, pain management in chronic pancreatitis, and enzyme replacement therapies.
symptoms: Upper abdominal pain radiating to back, fever, rapid pulse, nausea and vomiting, tenderness when touching the abdomen, jaundice, oily stools
riskFactors: Gallstones, excessive alcohol consumption, smoking, obesity, certain medications, high triglycerides, family history, autoimmune conditions, pancreatic duct abnormalities
treatment: Treatment includes fasting, IV fluids, pain management, and addressing underlying causes (gallstone removal, alcohol cessation). Clinical trials explore antioxidant therapies, protease inhibitors, and endoscopic treatments for chronic pancreatitis complications.
whyParticipate: Pancreatitis clinical trials help develop targeted therapies to reduce inflammation, manage pain, and prevent pancreatic damage progression.

faq.q: What are pancreatitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Pancreatitis clinical trials test new medications and endoscopic techniques to reduce pancreatic inflammation and manage chronic pain. Studies explore antioxidant therapies and enzyme replacement strategies.

faq.q: Who can participate in pancreatitis clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on pancreatitis type (acute or chronic), severity, underlying cause, and prior treatments. Some studies focus on hereditary or autoimmune pancreatitis.

faq.q: How much do pancreatitis clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with pancreatic function tests and imaging provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are pancreatitis clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close medical monitoring including pancreatic enzyme levels and imaging. All trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
description: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot that forms in a deep vein, usually in the legs, that can be life-threatening if it travels to the lungs. Clinical trials investigate novel anticoagulants, compression therapies, and thrombus removal techniques.
symptoms: Leg swelling, pain or tenderness, warmth over the affected area, red or discolored skin, visible surface veins, leg cramping (often in calf)
riskFactors: Prolonged immobility, surgery or trauma, cancer, pregnancy, obesity, smoking, oral contraceptives, age (over 60), inherited clotting disorders, family history
treatment: Treatment includes anticoagulant medications (warfarin, heparin, DOACs), compression stockings, and in severe cases, thrombectomy or thrombolysis. Clinical trials explore factor XI inhibitors with lower bleeding risk, novel reversal agents, and catheter-directed thrombolysis techniques.
whyParticipate: DVT clinical trials help develop safer anticoagulants with lower bleeding risk and improved strategies for preventing post-thrombotic syndrome.

faq.q: What are DVT clinical trials?
faq.a: DVT clinical trials test new blood thinners, clot removal techniques, and compression therapies to treat deep vein clots and prevent complications. Studies aim to reduce bleeding risk while maintaining effectiveness.

faq.q: Who can participate in DVT clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on clot location and extent, prior DVT history, bleeding risk factors, and underlying causes including genetic clotting disorders.

faq.q: How much do DVT clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$200 per visit with ultrasound monitoring and study medications provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are DVT clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close monitoring including regular blood tests and imaging. All trials have safety protocols to manage any bleeding complications.

name: Peripheral Artery Disease
description: Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) is a circulatory condition where narrowed arteries reduce blood flow to the limbs, typically the legs. Clinical trials investigate new revascularization techniques, drug-coated balloons, and medical therapies to improve walking distance and prevent amputation.
symptoms: Leg pain or cramping with walking (claudication), numbness or weakness in legs, cold feet, sores that do not heal, change in leg color, shiny leg skin, weak pulse in legs
riskFactors: Smoking, diabetes, obesity, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, age (over 50), family history, kidney disease, metabolic syndrome
treatment: Treatment includes cilostazol, antiplatelet therapy, statins, supervised exercise therapy, and revascularization (angioplasty, stenting, bypass surgery). Clinical trials explore novel anti-restenosis therapies, gene therapy for angiogenesis, and drug-coated balloon technologies.
whyParticipate: Peripheral artery disease clinical trials advance treatment options that can improve mobility, reduce amputation risk, and address systemic cardiovascular risk.

faq.q: What are peripheral artery disease clinical trials?
faq.a: PAD clinical trials test new medications, stents, and balloon technologies to improve leg circulation and relieve walking pain. Studies explore both medical therapies and minimally invasive revascularization approaches.

faq.q: Who can participate in peripheral artery disease clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on PAD severity (ankle-brachial index), walking distance limitations, and prior revascularization history. Studies often include patients with claudication or critical limb ischemia.

faq.q: How much do peripheral artery disease clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $75-$200 per visit with vascular imaging and study procedures provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are peripheral artery disease clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular vascular monitoring and cardiovascular assessments. All trials have independent safety oversight.

name: Eating Disorders
description: Eating disorders are serious mental health conditions characterized by persistent disturbances in eating behavior and body image. Clinical trials investigate psychotherapy approaches, pharmacotherapies, neuromodulation treatments, and digital interventions for anorexia, bulimia, and binge eating disorder.
symptoms: Severe food restriction, bingeing, purging, excessive exercise, distorted body image, extreme weight changes, obsession with food and calories, dental problems, gastrointestinal issues
riskFactors: Genetic predisposition, family history, age (adolescence and young adulthood), female gender, perfectionism, trauma, societal pressure, certain sports and professions
treatment: Treatment includes cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT-E), family-based treatment (FBT), nutritional counseling, and medications (SSRIs, lisdexamfetamine for binge eating). Clinical trials explore psychedelic-assisted therapy, neuromodulation techniques (TMS, DBS), and specialized digital therapeutics.
whyParticipate: Eating disorder clinical trials help develop more effective treatments with higher recovery rates and improved long-term outcomes.

faq.q: What are eating disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eating disorder clinical trials test new psychotherapies, medications, and neuromodulation treatments for anorexia, bulimia, and binge eating disorder. Studies explore both behavioral and biological approaches to recovery.

faq.q: Who can participate in eating disorder clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on eating disorder type and severity, BMI criteria, medical stability, and motivation for treatment. Studies may focus on specific diagnoses or age groups.

faq.q: How much do eating disorder clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$200 per visit with therapy sessions and nutritional counseling provided at no or reduced cost.

faq.q: Are eating disorder clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular medical and psychological monitoring. Safety protocols ensure participants maintain medical stability throughout the study.

name: Gallstones
description: Gallstones are hardened deposits of digestive fluid that form in the gallbladder, ranging in size from tiny to golf-ball sized. Clinical trials investigate non-surgical dissolution therapies, advanced laparoscopic techniques, and medical prevention strategies.
symptoms: Sudden intense abdominal pain (right upper quadrant), back pain between shoulder blades, right shoulder pain, nausea and vomiting, jaundice, fever with chills (indicates infection)
riskFactors: Female gender, age (over 40), obesity, pregnancy, rapid weight loss, high-fat and low-fiber diet, diabetes, certain medications, family history, Native American ethnicity
treatment: Treatment includes laparoscopic cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal), and less commonly, oral bile acid medications (ursodiol) for dissolution. Clinical trials explore single-incision laparoscopic techniques, natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES), and medications to prevent gallstone formation.
whyParticipate: Gallstone clinical trials advance surgical techniques for faster recovery and explore medical alternatives to surgery for gallstone management.

faq.q: What are gallstone clinical trials?
faq.a: Gallstone clinical trials test new surgical techniques, non-surgical dissolution therapies, and prevention strategies for gallbladder stones. Studies explore minimally invasive approaches with faster recovery.

faq.q: Who can participate in gallstone clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on stone size and number, gallbladder function, symptom frequency, and prior complications. Some studies focus on patients who are not surgical candidates.

faq.q: How much do gallstone clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with ultrasound monitoring and study procedures provided at no cost.

faq.q: Are gallstone clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive regular monitoring including ultrasound and blood work. All surgical trials follow strict safety protocols.

name: Leukemia
description: Leukemia is a cancer of blood-forming tissues including bone marrow and lymphatic system, causing abnormal white blood cell production. Clinical trials investigate targeted therapies, immunotherapies including CAR T-cell therapy, and combination treatment approaches.
symptoms: Fatigue, frequent infections, fever, easy bruising or bleeding, weight loss, swollen lymph nodes, bone pain, night sweats, shortness of breath
riskFactors: Age, smoking, family history, certain genetic disorders (Down syndrome), prior chemotherapy or radiation, exposure to benzene, certain blood disorders (MDS)
treatment: Treatment includes chemotherapy, targeted therapy (tyrosine kinase inhibitors, FLT3 inhibitors), immunotherapy (CAR T-cells, bispecific antibodies), and stem cell transplantation. Clinical trials explore novel targeted agents, allogeneic CAR T-cells, and combination immunotherapy strategies.
whyParticipate: Leukemia clinical trials offer access to groundbreaking immunotherapies and targeted treatments that are transforming survival rates for blood cancers.

faq.q: What are leukemia clinical trials?
faq.a: Leukemia clinical trials test new chemotherapy combinations, targeted therapies, and immunotherapies including CAR T-cell therapy to treat blood cancers. Studies focus on improving remission rates and reducing treatment toxicity.

faq.q: Who can participate in leukemia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on leukemia type (AML, ALL, CLL, CML), genetic markers, disease stage, prior treatments, and overall health status.

faq.q: How much do leukemia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Many trials focus on treatment access and may provide travel reimbursement plus a stipend of $50-$100 per visit.

faq.q: Are leukemia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close monitoring from oncology teams. All trials have safety committees and access to standard treatments is maintained.

name: Lymphoma
description: Lymphoma is a cancer of the lymphatic system affecting lymphocytes, with two main types: Hodgkin and Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Clinical trials investigate novel targeted agents, bispecific antibodies, CAR T-cell therapies, and personalized medicine approaches.
symptoms: Swollen lymph nodes (neck, armpits, groin), fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, fatigue, itching, chest pain or cough, abdominal pain
riskFactors: Age, gender, family history, certain infections (Epstein-Barr, HIV, Helicobacter pylori), immune disorders, autoimmune diseases, prior cancer treatment
treatment: Treatment includes chemotherapy, radiation, targeted therapy (BTK inhibitors, PI3K inhibitors), immunotherapy (rituximab, checkpoint inhibitors), CAR T-cell therapy, and stem cell transplantation. Clinical trials explore novel bispecific T-cell engagers, antibody-drug conjugates, and cellular immunotherapies.
whyParticipate: Lymphoma clinical trials provide access to innovative immunotherapies and targeted treatments that are achieving remarkable results in previously hard-to-treat cases.

faq.q: What are lymphoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Lymphoma clinical trials test new chemotherapy regimens, targeted therapies, and immunotherapies including CAR T-cells and bispecific antibodies. Studies aim to improve cure rates and reduce long-term side effects.

faq.q: Who can participate in lymphoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on lymphoma type (Hodgkin vs Non-Hodgkin), subtype (DLBCL, follicular, etc.), disease stage, prior treatments, and genetic markers.

faq.q: How much do lymphoma clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies by study. Participants typically receive travel reimbursement and may receive a stipend of $50-$150 per visit.

faq.q: Are lymphoma clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close oncological monitoring throughout treatment. All trials have independent safety oversight and ethical approval.

name: Melanoma
description: Melanoma is the most serious type of skin cancer, developing from pigment-producing melanocytes. Clinical trials investigate checkpoint immunotherapies, targeted therapies for BRAF-mutated melanoma, and innovative combination treatment strategies.
symptoms: New pigmented spot or existing mole that changes size, shape, or color, asymmetry or irregular borders, multiple colors, diameter larger than pencil eraser, evolution over time, ulceration or bleeding
riskFactors: UV exposure (sun and tanning beds), fair skin, many moles, family history, personal history of skin cancer, weakened immune system, age, male gender
treatment: Treatment includes surgical excision, checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab), targeted therapy for BRAF mutations (dabrafenib, trametinib), and radiation for advanced disease. Clinical trials explore neoadjuvant immunotherapy combinations, tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy, and novel vaccine approaches.
whyParticipate: Melanoma clinical trials have revolutionized treatment with immunotherapies that achieve durable responses, and continue pushing toward more effective combination strategies.

faq.q: What are melanoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Melanoma clinical trials test new immunotherapies, targeted therapies, and vaccine approaches to treat advanced skin cancer. Studies explore combination strategies to improve response rates and durability.

faq.q: Who can participate in melanoma clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on melanoma stage (localized, regional, or metastatic), BRAF mutation status, prior treatments, and overall health status.

faq.q: How much do melanoma clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants may receive $50-$150 per visit with travel reimbursement for extended treatment schedules.

faq.q: Are melanoma clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive close oncological monitoring including regular skin exams and imaging. All trials have independent safety committees.

name: Hernia
description: A hernia occurs when an organ pushes through an opening in the muscle or tissue that holds it in place, most commonly in the abdomen. Clinical trials investigate new mesh materials, laparoscopic and robotic repair techniques, and enhanced recovery protocols.
symptoms: Bulge or lump in the affected area, aching or burning sensation, discomfort with lifting or bending, heaviness or pressure, pain in the groin or scrotum (inguinal hernia)
riskFactors: Age, male gender, family history, obesity, heavy lifting, chronic cough, constipation, pregnancy, prior surgery, connective tissue disorders
treatment: Treatment typically involves surgical repair with or without mesh, using open, laparoscopic, or robotic approaches. Clinical trials explore biologic and synthetic mesh materials, tension-free repair techniques, and single-incision laparoscopic approaches for faster recovery.
whyParticipate: Hernia clinical trials advance surgical techniques and mesh technologies to reduce recurrence rates and improve postoperative recovery.

faq.q: What are hernia clinical trials?
faq.a: Hernia clinical trials test new surgical mesh materials, laparoscopic techniques, and recovery protocols for hernia repair. Studies aim to reduce recurrence rates, postoperative pain, and recovery time.

faq.q: Who can participate in hernia clinical trials?
faq.a: Eligibility depends on hernia type (inguinal, ventral, hiatal), size, symptom severity, and prior hernia repairs. Studies may compare different surgical approaches.

faq.q: How much do hernia clinical trials pay?
faq.a: Compensation varies. Participants typically receive $50-$150 per visit with surgical costs and follow-up care provided at reduced or no cost.

faq.q: Are hernia clinical trials safe?
faq.a: Yes, participants receive thorough pre- and post-operative monitoring. All surgical trials follow strict safety protocols with experienced surgeons.
